request.question: Vincent Cirelli is a special effects supervisor Known for his works in acclaimed films such as a 2008 horror film based on a 1984 short story by who?

title: Richard Bluff
Jason Snell (visual effects artist)
The Midnight Meat Train
Neil Corbould
Eric Brevig
Oliver Jones (visual effects artist)
Vincent Cirelli
Special effects supervisor
Nick Allder
Kaj Steveman
sentences: Richard Bluff is an English special effects supervisor.

Known for his works in Disney's visual effects company Industrial Light & Magic (ILM) as a digital matte artist and visual effects supervisor in acclaimed films such as "" (2005), "The Island" (2005), "Transformers" (2007-11), "Indiana Jones and the Kingdom of the Crystal Skull" (2008), "Star Trek" (2009), "Avatar" (2009), "The Avengers" (2012), "Cloud Atlas" (2012) "Pacific Rim" (2013), "The Big Short" (2015) and "Doctor Strange" (2016), for which he received an Academy Award for Best Visual Effects nomination at the 89th Academy Awards.
He previously worked at Blur Studio as digital artist.
Jason H. Snell is an American special effects supervisor known for his works in Disney's visual effects company Industrial Light & Magic (ILM).

Hammeck has worked as a production coordinator and VFX supervisor in films, "Men in Black" (1997), "Pirates of the Caribbean (film series)" (2003-07), "" (2005), "Indiana Jones and the Kingdom of the Crystal Skull" (2008), "Battleship" (2012), "Star Trek Into Darkness" (2013), "Tomorrowland" (2015), "Monster Hunt" (2015), "Deepwater Horizon" (2016), "Silence" (2016) and "Monster Hunt 2" (2018).
The Midnight Meat Train is a 2008 horror film based on Clive Barker's 1984 short story of the same name, which can be found in Volume One of Barker's collection "Books of Blood".
The film follows a photographer who attempts to track down a serial killer dubbed the "Subway Butcher", and discovers more than he bargained for under the city streets.
Neil Corbould (born 24 December 1962) is a British special effects supervisor best known for his work on major blockbuster films such as "Gladiator", "Saving Private Ryan", "" and "Black Hawk Down".
He is the brother of fellow special effects supervisor Chris Corbould and Paul Corbould.
Eric Brevig (born 1957) is an American film director and visual effects supervisor known for his work in several major theatrical films and television shows.
He was Visual Effects Supervisor and Second Unit Director on the 2001 Jerry Bruckheimer/Michael Bay action drama Pearl Harbor.
Oliver Jones is a British special effects supervisor.

Known for his works at LAIKA as a visual effects supervisor in acclaimed films such as "Coraline" (2009), "ParaNorman" (2012), and "Kubo and the Two Strings" for which he received Academy Award for Best Visual Effects nomination at 89th Academy Awards, that he shared with Steve Emerson, Brian McLean, and Brad Schiff.
Vincent Cirelli is a special effects supervisor.

Known for his works at Luma Pictures as a visual effects supervisor in acclaimed films such as "" (2007), "No Country for Old Men" (2007), "The Midnight Meat Train" (2008), "" (2008), "Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince" (2009), "The Book of Eli" (2010), "" (2011), "" (2012), "Prometheus" (2012), "The Avengers" (2012), "Saving Mr. Banks" (2013), "Guardians of the Galaxy" (2014), "" (2015), "In the Heart of the Sea" (2015), "Deadpool" (2015) and "Doctor Strange" (2016), for which he received an Academy Award for Best Visual Effects nomination at the 89th Academy Awards.

A special effects supervisor (also referred to as a special effects coordinator or SFX Supervisor) is an individual who works on a commercial, theater, television or film set creating special effects.
The supervisor generally is the department head who defers to the film's director and/or producers, and who is in charge of the entire special effects team.
Special effects include anything that is manual or mechanically manipulated (also called "practical effects" or in camera effects).
This may include the use of mechanized props, special effects makeup, props, scenery, scale models, pyrotechnics and atmospheric effects: creating physical wind, rain, fog, snow, clouds etc.
Nick Allder is an English special effects supervisor and coordinator.
He started his career as an assistant camera operator and moved, after eight years, into special effects.

He has won multiple awards, including an Academy Award for Best Visual Effects for the science-fiction horror film "Alien" (1979), and a BAFTA Award for Best Special Visual Effects for the science-fiction film "The Fifth Element" (1997).
Allder has worked on the set of high-grossing films such as the epic space opera "The Empire Strikes Back" (1980) and the historical drama film "Braveheart" (1995).
Kaj Steveman (born 1968 in Stockholm) is a Swedish visual effects supervisor.

He was the Founder and head of Fido Film, one of Sweden's most prominent special effects studios and is most known for his acclaimed work on the Swedish vampire films Let the Right One In and Frostbite.
He was also visual effects supervisor for and Storm and worked as assistant director in the cult film Evil Ed.
He worked as a make-up artist on The Hunters.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Special effects were designed and created by Special Effects Supervisor, Cassandra Baker.  The film is a sequel to the 2015 horror anthology "Volumes of Blood", both of which were filmed in Owensboro, Kentucky.
response.summary_1:
Primary topics: Vincent Cirelli; "The Midnight Meat Train" (2008 film)

- "Vincent Cirelli is a special effects supervisor." (passage [1])
- "Known for his works at Luma Pictures as a visual effects supervisor" (passage [1])
- Lists "'The Midnight Meat Train' (2008)" among his credited films (passage [1])
- Missing fact: the author of the "1984 short story" the 2008 film is based on is NOT given in the passages — bridge entity: "The Midnight Meat Train" (2008 film)

Next-hop retrieval (priority order):
1. Fetch "The Midnight Meat Train (film)" — inspect lede and "Production"/"Adaptation" sections for source/author details.
2. Fetch "The Midnight Meat Train (short story)" or short-story bibliographies — inspect publication year (1984) and author entry.
3. Search query: "The Midnight Meat Train short story 1984 author" (or "The Midnight Meat Train 1984 short story bibliography").
response.hop2_query: The Midnight Meat Train 1984 short story author
hop2_docs: The Midnight Meat Train | The Midnight Meat Train is a 2008 horror film based on Clive Barker's 1984 short story of the same name, which can be found in Volume One of Barker's collection "Books of Blood".  The film follows a photographer who attempts to track down a serial killer dubbed the "Subway Butcher", and discovers more than he bargained for under the city streets.

Midnight Library | The Midnight Library is a series of children's short story collections in the genre of horror fiction.  The author is variously given as Damien Graves (US) and Nick Shadow (UK).  The first, "Voices", was published in 2005.  The typical format is three stories per book.  There have also been omnibus editions.

Mathieu Cailler | Mathieu Cailler (born June 16, 1984) is a French-American writer of poetry and prose.  A graduate of Vermont College of Fine Arts, he has been a finalist for the "Glimmer Train" New Writers Award, the New Rivers Press American Fiction Prize, and the "Carve Magazine" Raymond Carver Short Story Contest.  He is also the recipient of a Short Story America Prize for Short Fiction and a Shakespeare Award for Poetry.  He is the author of "Clotheslines" (Red Bird Press), "Shhh" (ELJ Publications), and the collection of short stories, "Loss Angeles" (Short Story America Press).

The Train (short story) | "The Train" is an early short story by the American author Flannery O'Connor.  It is one of the six stories included in O'Connor's 1947 master's thesis "The Geranium: A Collection of Short Stories" and was published in "The Sewanee Review" in 1948.  It later appeared in the 1971 collection "The Complete Stories".  O'Connor revised this story into the first chapter of her novel, "Wise Blood".
Vitamins (short story) | "Vitamins" is a short story by American author Raymond Carver.  It was originally published in 1984, in his collection titled "Cathedral."
Midnight at the Lost and Found | Midnight at the Lost and Found is a 1983 album by Meat Loaf.  This would be the final Meat Loaf release under Epic Records until "The Very Best of Meat Loaf" (1998).

John McManus (author) | John McManus is an American novelist and short story writer.  He is the author of a novel, "Bitter Milk," and the short story collections "Born on a Train" and "Stop Breakin Down."  In recognition of "Stop Breakin Down," McManus became the youngest ever recipient of the Whiting Award.
hop2_titles: Midnight at the Lost and Found
response.summary_2: Vincent Cirelli's credited film The Midnight Meat Train (2008) is based on a 1984 short story by Clive Barker. Clive Barker's 1984 short story of the same name appears in Volume One of his collection Books of Blood.
